Document [14] (Title: Central Sleep Apnea -- Continuing Education Activity) Central sleep apnea is a breathing disorder in which a person experiences apnea episodes are alternating with normal breathing. Patients may observe abrupt awakenings accompanied by shortness of breath, insomnia, and excessive daytime sleepiness, along with difficulty concentrating and mood changes. Central sleep apnea is different from obstructive sleep apnea, in which there is abnormal breathing due to upper airway obstruction. Central sleep apnea can result from heart failure, stroke, high altitude. This activity reviews pathophysiology and diagnostic and therapeutic modalities available for managing central sleep apnea and highlights the interprofessional team's role in managing the condition better.

Document [20] (Title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Continuing Education Activity) Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is a sleep disorder characterized by repeated episodes of complete (apnea) or partial (hypopnea) collapse of the upper airway, causing oxygen desaturation or sleep arousal. This disruption leads to fragmented, nonrestorative sleep. Symptoms include loud, disruptive snoring, witnessed apneas, and excessive daytime sleepiness. This condition has significant implications for cardiovascular health, mental well-being, quality of life, and driving safety. Diagnosis involves polysomnography or home sleep apnea testing. Treatment options include continuous positive airway pressure therapy, oral appliances, and surgical interventions.

Document [22] (Title: Sleep Apnea Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity) Sleep apnea syndrome (SAS) is defined by frequent episodes of apnea and hypopnea associated with symptoms such as excessive daytime and cardiovascular morbidity and mortality. Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome is a condition in which there is a dynamic collapse of upper airway tissues during sleep. This may result in recurrent respiratory-related events and both short-term symptomatic consequences and long-term physiologic consequences. This activity reviews the evaluation, workup, and management of adult patients with obstructive sleep apnea and highlights the role of the healthcare team in evaluating and treating these patients.

Document [30] (Title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea -- Evaluation) Adult patients with unexplained daytime or sleep-related symptoms, such as excessive sleepiness, fatigue, or unrefreshing sleep, should be evaluated for sleep apnea. However, universal screening for OSA is not recommended in asymptomatic patients, except for those at risk due to occupational hazards, such as drivers or pilots. [35] [36] In addition, due to the high prevalence of OSA and its disease burden, patients with specific comorbidities, including refractory atrial fibrillation, resistant hypertension, and a history of stroke, should also be screened for sleep apnea, regardless of symptoms. Document [16] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936) Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

Together, these interrelationships clarify how drug classes, individual agents, chemical families, therapeutic uses, mechanisms of action, contraindications and adverse‑effect profiles are organized, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, anticipating co‑occurring risks and monitoring relevant clinical outcomes.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1518) Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia). The condition الاِختِنَاق (apnea) is also mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, linking apnea to the same hypoxemic state seen in sickle cell disease. Together, these relationships underscore that patients with داء الكريات المنجلية are at risk for severe respiratory complications, including hypoxemia and apnea, and highlight the importance of vigilant respiratory monitoring and oxygen management in their care.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1162) The syndrome “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)” is characterized as a collection of related neuropsychiatric manifestations. It includes “اضطراب التصرف” as one of its constituent features, and “اللامبالاة” is also identified as a member of this syndrome. In addition, the SMQ encompasses deficits in attention (“خلل في الانتباه”), reduced ability to maintain focus (“ضعف التركيز”), and an early presentation of excessive daytime sleepiness (“فرط النوم في النهار الأولي”). Together, these elements define the clinical profile of “الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ)”, indicating that patients with this condition may exhibit behavior disorder, apathy, attention and concentration problems, and initial excessive daytime sleepiness as interrelated components of the disorder.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1865) Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is classified as a type of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder). The broader category اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ is itself a subcategory of الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia). In clinical terminology, الاِختِنَاق is mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating that asphyxia reflects a state of reduced oxygen concentration in the blood (hypoxemia). Understanding this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians recognize that adult respiratory failure and arrest represent specific manifestations of respiratory disorders that ultimately lead to or are associated with systemic hypoxemia.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 702) clinically appropriate and economically evaluable descriptors, supports precise risk assessment and patient counseling for interventions with potential adverse effects, and facilitates comprehensive, multilingual documentation across multiple specialties.
